subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear purpose and focus, reinforced consistently through each section.
The research question is sharply delimited, explicitly stated, and drives the methodology and analysis.
The methodology is complete with an appropriate range of relevant sources and methods, including the construction and explanation of a composite index.
weaknesses: Occasional misuse of terminology (e.g., âeastern blocâ) and some redundant phrasing detract slightly from absolute precision.
Justification for benchmark city selection is brief and could be more thoroughly explained.
Variable categorization and integration of the Indiana case study lack full alignment with the research question.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed topic communication and methodology at the top of the 5â6 band and the research question in the 6 band, yielding an overall strong performance. Minor lapses prevent the top mark, so a 5 out of 6 appropriately reflects consistent clarity, focus and methodological completeness while acknowledging small shortcomings.

strengths: Use of specialist terminology (e.g., BPO, SEZ, demographic transition) is generally accurate and consistently applied.
Subject knowledge of urban geography and the BPO/SSC sector is clear, with accurate description of labour, demographic and infrastructure factors.
Definitions are provided and applied in context, and international examples add valuable context.
weaknesses: Reliance on informal online sources (YouTube, blogs) weakens academic credibility.
Theoretical concepts (agglomeration economies, coreâperiphery interactions) are mentioned but not analysed in depth.
Minor terminological imprecision (e.g., âEastern bloc Polish citiesâ) and occasional grammatical slips blur precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Terminology and subject knowledge both fall in the 3â4 band. While the work demonstrates clear understanding and effective use of concepts, it lacks the depth, source rigour and theoretical sophistication required for a 5â6 band, making 4 out of 6 the appropriate score.

strengths: Primary and secondary data are purposefully selected and systematically linked to the research question.
Analysis of variables and composite index is well structured, with mostly supported conclusions drawn from data.
The argument is coherent and persuasive, synthesising quantitative findings into clear policy recommendations.
weaknesses: Discussion of measurement error, data interpolation and weighting choices is limited.
Engagement with theoretical frameworks (growth pole, dependency theory) is underdeveloped.
Counter-arguments and alternative explanations receive only brief treatment.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research application and analysis both align with the 7â9 band, and the overall argument is coherent but not fully exemplary. An 8 out of 12 reflects good, well-supported critical thinking with room for deeper theoretical integration and methodological reflection.

strengths: The report follows a clear, conventional structure with a comprehensive table of contents and logically ordered sections.
Visual elements (maps, tables, graphs) are captioned and support understanding.
Page numbering and section numbering are consistently applied, aiding navigation.
weaknesses: Layout suffers from misaligned tables, inconsistent font sizing and oversized URLs.
Mathematical notation is not uniformly formatted and appendices are not systematically cited.
Cross-referencing to tables and appendices is incomplete, reducing ease of reference.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure meets the top band descriptor for presentation but layout considerations fall into the lower band. Averaging the two strands yields 3 out of 4, reflecting strong overall organisation tempered by noticeable formatting inconsistencies.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Primary and secondary data are purposefully selected and systematically incorporated into the BPOAI, ensuring that every variable directly addresses the research question. Extensive municipal statistics, demographic yearbooks, and office-market reports are mined to populate the index and provide contextual evidence in the discussion.  

There is only minor mis-alignment between listed variables and those ultimately analysed, and some concerns about the provenance of a few informal sources. Nevertheless, the overall linkage between data and inquiry remains clear and well-sustained, matching the descriptor of âgood with clear relevanceâ. A mid-band (7â9) score of 8 is therefore awarded.

The student constructs a composite BPO Attractiveness Index (BPOAI) built from nine relative and three absolute variables, gathered from official statistics, municipal reports and secondary literature. The rescaling formula is explained and benchmark cities are justified as national leaders, illustrating sound methodological planning.  

Nonetheless, several variables listed in the âabsoluteâ table are later treated as relative; justifications for using approximated or interpolated data are brief, and the essay gives limited reflection on reliability or uncertainty introduced by rescaling. These gaps prevent the work from demonstrating the full rigour expected for the top mark, but overall the methodology remains complete and appropriate, meriting a solid 5 in the 5â6 band.

The essay displays substantial knowledge of urban geography, demographic transition, and the BPO/SSC sector. The student accurately describes factors such as labour costs, demographic structures, and infrastructure in shaping location decisions, and integrates Polish and international examples (e.g., Manila) to contextualise findings.  

However, some explanations remain largely descriptiveâfor instance, location theory or agglomeration economies are mentioned implicitly but not analysed in depth. Reliance on several non-academic web sources (blogs, YouTube videos) also weakens academic credibility. These issues place the work at the upper end of the 3â4 band, just short of âexcellentâ mastery, warranting a score of 4.

Visual elementsâmaps, tables and graphsâare present, captioned and generally legible. Page numbering and a contents page are included, fulfilling basic EE conventions. However, several layout issues persist: mis-aligned table cells, oversized or breaking URLs, inconsistent font sizing, and mathematical notation that is not uniformly formatted. Appendices are not systematically cited in the main body, reducing ease of reference.  

Because layout principles are applied only partially and with noticeable inconsistencies, the work aligns with the 1â2 band for layout; a score of 2 is appropriate.

Throughout the essay the student employs specialist termsâBPO, SEZ, natural increase, offshoring, back-office/front-office, demographic transitionâgenerally accurately and in appropriate contexts. Definitions are provided early in the introduction and are applied consistently in later sections.  

Nonetheless, occasional lapses (e.g., interchangeable use of âeastern blocâ for âeastern Poland,â or minor grammatical slips) momentarily blur precision, and deeper theoretical concepts (e.g., coreâperiphery interactions) are only lightly touched upon. The overall use of terminology is therefore âmostly accurateâ rather than fully sophisticated, fitting a band 3â4 with a score of 4.

The student presents a highly focused topicâmeasuring and comparing the competitiveness of three eastern Polish cities in the national BPO landscapeâthat is introduced in the title, abstract and earlyâsection aims. Each subsequent chapter (introduction, methodology, results, discussion) clearly reinforces this focus, and key terms such as âcompetitivenessâ and âBPO attractivenessâ are employed consistently.  

Minor lapsesâe.g., occasional misuse of the term âeastern bloc,â and some redundant phrasingâslightly detract from absolute precision, but overall communication remains clear, purposeful and logically sustained across 4 000 words. These small shortcomings keep the work at the lower end of the 5â6 band, warranting a score of 5.

The discussion is logically organised city-by-city, constantly referencing index scores and individual variables to build a coherent argument about relative competitiveness. The narrative remains focused on the research question and synthesises findings into a persuasive ranking, culminating in policy recommendations.  

Yet, sections occasionally repeat points, some assumptions (e.g., ease of policy implementation) are uncritically accepted, and counter-arguments or alternative explanations (e.g., qualitative factors, investor perceptions) are only briefly noted. These minor weaknesses reduce the depth of critical engagement, placing the work at the lower end of the 7â9 band; a score of 7 is suitable.

The student conducts multi-year comparative analysis, interpreting trends in each of the nine variables and the composite BPOAI. Conclusions about each cityâs competitiveness are mostly backed by data from tables and graphs, and links between indicators and economic attractiveness are clearly articulated.  

However, treatment of measurement error, data interpolation and weighting choices is limited; some causal claims (e.g., policy impacts) are asserted rather than evidenced. These shortcomings prevent an âexcellent, fully supportedâ evaluation, but the work comfortably fulfils the âgood with mostly supported conclusionsâ descriptor within the 7â9 band; a score of 8 is justified.

The essay follows a conventional EE structureâabstract, introduction, methodology, results, discussion, conclusion, bibliography and appendicesâwith clear subsection numbering that mirrors the table of contents. Figures, tables and appendices are generally well integrated, aiding reader navigation and supporting the line of argument.  

Although some cross-referencing to appendices is missing, the overall organisation consistently supports understanding, satisfying the top band (3-4) for structure; a score of 4 is awarded.

The research questionââHow competitive are BiaÅystok, Lublin and RzeszÃ³w as largest eastern Polish BPO centers on a national scale?ââis explicitly stated, sharply delimited to three case-study cities and a defined sector, and clearly drives data selection, methodology and discussion. Its wording directs a comparative, measurable investigation rather than a narrative description, demonstrating full focus and relevance to the discipline of economic geography.  

Because the RQ is neither excessively broad nor ambiguous and underpins all analytical stages, it fully satisfies the descriptor for the top band; therefore a score of 6 within the 5â6 band is appropriate.

annotations.comment: Figure 1 caption omits the date and source details; include full citation and date of adaptation to meet presentation standards and clarify provenance.
The research question is clearly stated, sharply focused on competitiveness of three cities, and effectively anchors the subsequent methodology and analysis.
The title clearly communicates the focus on comparing BPO sector development in eastern Polish cities within a national context, establishing precise purpose and focus for the investigation.
Reliance on YouTube sources for economic data weakens academic credibility; prioritize peer-reviewed literature and official statistics to support subject knowledge.
Inverse rescaling formula $$\frac{obs - max}{min - max}$$ is introduced without clear derivation; elaborate on its origin and interpretational implications.
Interpretation of student share per 1000 inhabitants highlights trends but lacks context on tertiary education policies; integrate policy analysis for depth.
Discussion of RzeszÃ³wâs infrastructure benefit is descriptive; strengthen argument by linking to specific economic geography frameworks (e.g. growth pole theory).
Methodology description names benchmarks but omits justification of their selection; explain why Warsaw, KrakÃ³w and WrocÅaw serve as appropriate comparators.
Math formula should be formatted in LaTeX for consistency: e.g., $$\frac{obs - min}{max - min}$$ to improve clarity of presentation.
Variable selection list lacks hierarchy; structure bullets to directly map to push versus pull factors for clearer methodology linkage.
Including the word count at the start of the document meets IB formatting requirements and demonstrates attention to administrative conventions.
The table of contents is comprehensive and mirrors section numbering accurately, supporting clear navigation through the reportâs structure.
Font sizes and styling in the table of contents vary noticeably, creating inconsistency in presentation; standardize font and spacing for professional layout.
The term âEastern bloc Polish citiesâ inaccurately invokes Cold War terminology; use precise regional descriptors (e.g. eastern Poland) to maintain terminological accuracy.
Definitions of BPO types lack depth and theoretical context; integrate urban economic geography concepts to strengthen use of terminology and conceptual understanding.
Table 1 combines narrative and tabular content inconsistently; separate explanatory text from tables to uphold presentation clarity.
Discussion of case cities lacks engagement with coreâperiphery theory; incorporate theoretical models (e.g. dependency theory) to deepen critical analysis.
The narrative on Manila remains largely descriptive; connect its BPO growth to agglomeration economies or location theory to enhance conceptual application.
Figure 2âs caption lacks publication date; ensure all visual elements include complete metadata (author, date) for accurate referencing.
Heavy use of informal online articles and videos demands greater source evaluation; critically appraise their reliability and potential bias.
The Indiana case study is interesting but loosely tied to the research question; better integrate this example within the BPO competitiveness framework.
Results narrative refers broadly to nine tables without explicit table numbers; add precise cross-references to support reader navigation.
The list describing each variable overlaps with discussion; consider relocating these interpretative remarks to the analysis section for coherence.
